title: Stacked-Edge Plywood Picture Frame
summary: A picture frame built from ripped plywood strips, showcasing the striped stacked-ply edge grain as the design feature.
description:
A normal picture frame hides plywood if it uses it at all; this one is built specifically to show it off. Ripping strips off a sheet of plywood exposes the edge grain — the alternating stripes of veneer layers — and that pattern becomes the entire visual feature of the frame instead of something disguised with edge banding.

Because the striping is the point, **plywood choice matters more here than in almost any other project** — a cheap sheet with inconsistent veneer thickness looks messy once ripped and mitered, while a good Baltic birch sheet gives clean, even stripes.

Sized here for an 8x10 photo, but the strip-and-rabbet method scales to any frame size — just adjust the miter lengths.

tools.note: Rips the strips and can cut the rabbet.
Alternative way to cut the rabbet with a rabbeting bit.

materials.name: Hardwood plywood (e.g. Baltic birch), partial sheet

cutList.note: Mitered ends; adjust length to your photo size.
Mitered ends; adjust length to your photo size.

steps.title: Rip the strips
steps.body: Rip the plywood into strips about 3/4in wide on the table saw — use more strips for a wider frame profile.
materials: Hardwood plywood (e.g. Baltic birch), partial sheet

steps.title: Cut the rabbet
steps.body: Rout or table-saw a rabbet along the inside back edge of each strip, sized to hold your glass and backing thickness.

steps.title: Miter to length
steps.body: Miter the strips to length for your chosen photo size.

steps.title: Glue up the frame
steps.body: Glue and clamp the mitered corners together, checking each corner for square before the glue sets.

steps.title: Assemble and hang
steps.body: Drop in the glass, photo, and backing, attach the backing clips, then add a sawtooth hanger to the back.
